questions.explanation: Cloud KMS automatic rotation creates new key versions on schedule. Cloud Asset Inventory provides an organization-wide view of all KMS keys and their properties. A Cloud Monitoring alerting policy on key rotation metrics ensures non-compliant keys are detected and flagged.

questions.question: A company needs to implement data loss prevention across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and Datastore simultaneously with consistent policies. Which Cloud DLP feature enables this?
options: DLP job triggers that can be configured to automatically scan multiple data repositories on a schedule using the same inspection templates
Manual inspection of each repository separately
VPC Service Controls for DLP
Organization Policy constraints for DLP
questions.explanation: DLP job triggers automate scanning across Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and Datastore. Inspection templates define the infoTypes and rules to apply, and can be shared across multiple triggers ensuring consistent detection policies. Results are stored centrally for unified reporting.

questions.question: By default, all data at rest in Google Cloud is encrypted. What type of encryption does Google use by default?
options: Google-managed encryption keys (server-side encryption with AES-256)
No encryption by default
Customer-managed encryption keys
Client-side encryption
questions.explanation: Google Cloud automatically encrypts all data at rest using AES-256 with Google-managed encryption keys. This is applied to all services without any configuration needed. Customers can optionally use CMEK, CSEK, or EKM for additional key control.

questions.question: What is Cloud KMS used for?
options: Creating, managing, and using cryptographic keys for encrypting and signing data
Managing Kubernetes clusters
Monitoring service health
Deploying applications
questions.explanation: Cloud Key Management Service (Cloud KMS) is a managed service for creating, importing, and managing cryptographic keys. It supports symmetric and asymmetric keys for encryption, decryption, signing, and verification, with multiple protection levels (software, HSM, external).

questions.question: What are the three key protection levels available in Cloud KMS?
options: SOFTWARE, HSM, and EXTERNAL
LOW, MEDIUM, and HIGH
BASIC, STANDARD, and PREMIUM
AES, RSA, and ECC
questions.explanation: Cloud KMS offers three protection levels: SOFTWARE (keys protected by software), HSM (keys stored in FIPS 140-2 Level 3 certified hardware security modules), and EXTERNAL (keys stored in a customer-managed external key management system via EKM).

questions.question: What is Google Cloud Secret Manager used for?
options: Storing, managing, and accessing sensitive data like API keys, passwords, and certificates
Encrypting data at rest
Managing IAM policies
Monitoring application performance
questions.explanation: Secret Manager is a managed service for securely storing and accessing secrets such as API keys, database passwords, TLS certificates, and other sensitive configuration. It provides versioning, automatic rotation, and IAM-based access control.

questions.question: A microservices application has 20 services that each need database credentials. How should the credentials be managed securely?
options: Store credentials in Secret Manager and grant each service's service account the secretmanager.secretAccessor role on only the secrets it needs
Hard-code credentials in each service's configuration file
Store credentials in environment variables on each VM
Use a shared database account for all services
questions.explanation: Secret Manager with per-secret IAM access control ensures each service can only access its own credentials. Combined with dedicated service accounts per service, this implements least privilege. Secret Manager also supports automatic rotation and version pinning.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud feature allows you to encrypt data in use, not just at rest and in transit?
options: Confidential Computing (Confidential VMs and Confidential GKE Nodes)
Cloud KMS encryption
Cloud Armor
VPC Service Controls
questions.explanation: Confidential Computing encrypts data in use (while being processed in memory) using hardware-based trusted execution environments (AMD SEV or Intel TDX). Confidential VMs encrypt VM memory so even Google's infrastructure operators cannot access data being processed.

questions.question: A company processes sensitive financial data and needs assurance that the data is encrypted even in RAM during processing. Which compute option should they use?
options: Confidential VMs, which use AMD SEV or Intel TDX to encrypt VM memory with a per-VM key
Standard Compute Engine instances with disk encryption
Cloud Functions with CMEK
App Engine with client-side encryption
questions.explanation: Confidential VMs use hardware-based memory encryption (AMD SEV, Intel TDX) to encrypt the VM's memory with a per-VM key that is not accessible to the hypervisor or other VMs. This protects data in use — the third pillar of data protection alongside at-rest and in-transit.

questions.question: What is Binary Authorization in Google Cloud?
options: A deploy-time security control that ensures only trusted container images are deployed to GKE or Cloud Run
A tool for compiling binary code
An authentication mechanism for binary protocols
A storage format for encrypted binaries
questions.explanation: Binary Authorization enforces deploy-time policies that require container images to be signed by trusted authorities (attestors) before they can be deployed to GKE or Cloud Run. This ensures only verified, approved images from trusted registries run in production.

questions.question: A company wants to ensure that only container images that have passed vulnerability scanning are deployed to their GKE cluster. Which combination of services achieves this?
options: Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning, Binary Authorization with attestation based on scan results, and a GKE admission policy requiring attestation
Manual image review before deployment
Cloud Armor WAF rules for containers
IAM policies restricting image pulls
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry scans images for vulnerabilities, and results can trigger a Binary Authorization attestation if no critical vulnerabilities are found. GKE's Binary Authorization admission controller then only allows deployment of attested images, creating an automated security gate.

questions.question: How does Secret Manager handle secret rotation?
options: It supports versioning and can trigger a Pub/Sub notification or Cloud Function to perform automatic rotation of the secret value
It automatically changes all secret values every 90 days
It only supports manual rotation
It delegates rotation to Cloud KMS
questions.explanation: Secret Manager supports automatic rotation through Pub/Sub notifications or Cloud Function triggers. When a rotation is due, a notification is sent that triggers a function to generate a new secret value, update the external system, and add the new value as a new version.

questions.question: A Cloud KMS key has multiple versions. How does key rotation work with CMEK-encrypted data?
options: New encryption operations use the primary (latest) key version, while previously encrypted data remains encrypted with its original key version and can still be decrypted
All previously encrypted data is automatically re-encrypted with the new version
Old key versions are immediately destroyed after rotation
Key rotation requires manually re-encrypting all data
questions.explanation: Cloud KMS key rotation creates a new primary version for new encrypt operations. Previously encrypted data retains its original key version reference and can be decrypted without re-encryption. Old key versions remain active for decryption. This is called 'envelope encryption with key versioning'.

questions.question: What is Certificate Authority Service (CAS) in Google Cloud?
options: A managed service for creating and managing private certificate authorities (CAs) and issuing TLS/SSL certificates
A service for purchasing public SSL certificates
A certificate pinning service
A DNS validation service for Let's Encrypt
questions.explanation: Certificate Authority Service (CAS) is a managed private CA that enables you to create root and subordinate CAs, issue X.509 certificates for TLS/mTLS, and manage certificate lifecycle. It is used for service-to-service authentication, device identity, and internal PKI.

questions.question: A microservices architecture requires mutual TLS (mTLS) between all services. Which Google Cloud service issues the internal certificates?
options: Certificate Authority Service (CAS) for issuing internal certificates, or GKE Workload Certificates for mesh-based mTLS
Certificate Manager (for external certificates only)
Cloud Armor SSL policies
Cloud Load Balancer managed certificates
questions.explanation: CAS provides a private CA for issuing internal certificates used in mTLS. For GKE-based microservices, GKE Workload Certificates or Anthos Service Mesh can automatically provision and rotate mTLS certificates for pod-to-pod communication.

questions.question: A company uses Cloud KMS to encrypt data in multiple services (Cloud Storage, BigQuery, Compute Engine). They need to audit all KMS key usage across the organization. Which approach provides this?
options: Cloud Audit Logs for Cloud KMS capture every encrypt, decrypt, and administrative operation with the caller identity, key, and timestamp
Cloud Monitoring KMS metrics only
Cloud Asset Inventory key listing
Manual review of each service's encryption settings
questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs automatically capture all Cloud KMS API calls including encrypt, decrypt, createCryptoKey, and administrative operations. Each log entry includes the caller identity, key resource name, operation, and timestamp, providing a complete audit trail of all key usage.

questions.question: A Secret Manager secret is accessed by 50 Cloud Functions. The security team needs to rotate the database password without any downtime. How should this be implemented?
options: Create the new password as a new secret version, update the database to accept both old and new passwords, update the Cloud Functions to reference the 'latest' version alias, then disable the old version
Delete the old secret and create a new one
Change the password and redeploy all functions simultaneously
Keep using the same password to avoid disruption
questions.explanation: Zero-downtime rotation uses a dual-password strategy: the new version is added while the old remains active. Functions using the 'latest' alias automatically get the new version on their next Secret Manager call. After all functions have picked up the new version, the old version is disabled.

questions.question: What attestation report does a Confidential VM provide, and what does it prove?
options: A hardware-signed attestation report from the AMD SEV firmware proving the VM is running in an encrypted memory environment with the expected launch measurement
A Google-signed certificate of encryption
A Cloud Audit Log entry confirming VM creation
An IAM policy attestation
questions.explanation: Confidential VMs generate a hardware-signed attestation report from the AMD SEV (or Intel TDX) firmware. This report proves that the VM is running in a trusted execution environment with encrypted memory and that the firmware/configuration matches the expected values (launch measurement).

questions.question: A CI/CD pipeline builds container images and pushes them to Artifact Registry. The security team wants Binary Authorization to automatically attest images that pass both vulnerability scanning AND unit tests. How should this be architected?
options: Create two attestors (one for vulnerability scan results, one for test results), and configure the Binary Authorization policy to require both attestations before allowing deployment
Use a single attestor that combines both checks
Rely on manual approval for each deployment
Skip attestation and use image digest pinning only
questions.explanation: Multiple attestors provide defense in depth. The vulnerability scan attestor signs images with no critical CVEs, and the test attestor signs images that pass all tests. The Binary Authorization policy requires BOTH attestations, ensuring no image is deployed without meeting both security gates.

questions.question: What is envelope encryption, and how does Cloud KMS use it?
options: Data is encrypted with a local Data Encryption Key (DEK), and the DEK is encrypted with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) stored in Cloud KMS
Data is encrypted twice with two different KMS keys
Data is encrypted and placed inside an encrypted envelope file
Cloud KMS sends the encryption key to the application for local encryption
questions.explanation: Envelope encryption uses a two-tier key hierarchy: a local DEK encrypts the data (fast, can handle large volumes), and Cloud KMS encrypts the DEK with a KEK (only the small DEK needs to be sent to KMS). This combines KMS security with high-performance local encryption.

questions.question: What advantage does Secret Manager have over storing secrets in environment variables?
options: Secret Manager provides versioning, access control, audit logging, and automatic rotation — none of which are available with plain environment variables
Secret Manager is faster to read
Secret Manager is less expensive
Environment variables are more secure
questions.explanation: Secret Manager provides centralized management with fine-grained IAM access control (who can read which secrets), version management (rollback capability), audit logging (who accessed what), automatic rotation, and encryption at rest — none of which environment variables offer.

questions.question: Which Google Cloud service scans container images stored in Artifact Registry for known vulnerabilities?
options: Artifact Analysis (Container Scanning), which automatically scans images for OS and language package vulnerabilities
Cloud Armor
Security Command Center Web Scanner
Cloud DLP
questions.explanation: Artifact Analysis (formerly Container Analysis) automatically scans container images pushed to Artifact Registry for known vulnerabilities in OS packages and language packages. Results include CVE IDs, severity, and fixed-in versions, and integrate with Binary Authorization and SCC.

questions.question: What is the break-glass mechanism in Binary Authorization?
options: A policy override that allows deploying an unattested image in emergency situations, while creating an audit log entry for review
A way to permanently disable Binary Authorization
A mechanism to bypass all security controls
An emergency deletion of all attestations
questions.explanation: Break-glass is an escape hatch in Binary Authorization that allows deploying images that don't meet the attestation policy during emergencies. It creates a detailed audit log entry and can trigger an alert. This ensures urgent deployments aren't blocked while maintaining accountability.

questions.question: A company needs to ensure that a Cloud KMS key can ONLY be used for encrypting data in a specific Cloud Storage bucket, not for any other purpose. How can this be enforced?
options: Use IAM conditions on the Cloud KMS key's encrypt permission that restrict usage to requests originating from the specific Cloud Storage bucket's resource name
Create a dedicated KMS keyring for the bucket
Use a CSEK instead of CMEK
Restrict the key to a single algorithm
questions.explanation: IAM conditions on the KMS CryptoKey Encrypter role can include resource-based conditions that restrict which resources can use the key. By specifying the Cloud Storage bucket's resource name as a condition, encrypt operations from other resources are denied.

questions.question: An organization is implementing a zero-trust service mesh. They need automated certificate issuance, mTLS enforcement, and certificate rotation for 500+ microservices. Which architecture is recommended?
options: Certificate Authority Service as the root CA, with Anthos Service Mesh handling automatic mTLS certificate issuance, enforcement, and rotation for all service-to-service communication
Manually generate and distribute certificates to each service
Use self-signed certificates with long expiration
Use Cloud Load Balancer for all service communication
questions.explanation: CAS provides the root CA for the trust hierarchy. Anthos Service Mesh (or Istio) automatically requests short-lived certificates from CAS for each service, enforces mTLS for all inter-service traffic, and rotates certificates before expiration — all without application code changes.

questions.question: What is the difference between Confidential VMs and Shielded VMs?
options: Shielded VMs protect against boot-level tampering (Secure Boot, vTPM, Integrity Monitoring); Confidential VMs additionally encrypt memory to protect data during processing
They are the same feature
Shielded VMs encrypt memory; Confidential VMs protect boot integrity
Shielded VMs are for GKE; Confidential VMs are for Compute Engine only
questions.explanation: Shielded VMs protect the boot process (Secure Boot, vTPM for measured boot, Integrity Monitoring) to prevent rootkits and bootkits. Confidential VMs build on this by adding hardware-based memory encryption (AMD SEV/Intel TDX), protecting data even while it is being processed in RAM.

questions.question: A security audit reveals that some Secret Manager secrets have not been accessed in over 6 months, potentially indicating orphaned credentials. How can the security engineer identify and remediate these?
options: Query Cloud Audit Logs for Secret Manager access events, identify secrets with no recent accessSecretVersion calls, and investigate whether they can be disabled or deleted
Delete all secrets older than 6 months
Rotate all secrets simultaneously
Disable Secret Manager access logging
questions.explanation: Cloud Audit Logs record every secret access (secretmanager.versions.access). Querying for secrets with no recent access calls reveals potentially orphaned credentials. These should be investigated — if no longer needed, the secret versions should be disabled (not immediately destroyed) to allow recovery if needed.

questions.question: What is Artifact Registry in Google Cloud?
options: A managed service for storing, managing, and securing container images, language packages, and OS packages
A code repository service
A CI/CD pipeline service
A deployment management tool
questions.explanation: Artifact Registry is Google Cloud's universal package manager that stores container images (Docker, OCI), language packages (npm, Maven, Python), and OS packages (apt, yum). It provides vulnerability scanning, access control, and integration with Binary Authorization and Cloud Build.

questions.question: How does Cloud KMS encrypt data in transit between the client and the KMS service?
options: All communication with Cloud KMS uses TLS (HTTPS) for encryption in transit, and KMS operations use authenticated encryption
Data is sent unencrypted and encrypted at the KMS service
A VPN tunnel is required for KMS access
Client-side encryption is required before sending to KMS
questions.explanation: All Cloud KMS API calls use TLS (HTTPS), ensuring data is encrypted in transit. The encrypt/decrypt operations themselves use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM), which provides both confidentiality and integrity. For additional protection, VPC Service Controls can restrict KMS access to private networks.

questions.question: A company uses Certificate Manager (not CAS) for their external-facing load balancers. What is Certificate Manager's primary function?
options: Provisioning, managing, and auto-renewing public TLS certificates for Google Cloud load balancers
Managing private CA hierarchies
Issuing mTLS certificates for internal services
Storing SSH keys for VM access
questions.explanation: Certificate Manager handles public TLS certificate lifecycle for external load balancers. It supports Google-managed certificates (automatic provisioning and renewal), self-managed certificates (user-uploaded), and DNS authorization for wildcard certificates. It differs from CAS which handles private/internal PKI.

questions.question: Which GKE security feature ensures that only verified, unmodified node images are used when nodes boot?
options: Shielded GKE Nodes with Secure Boot and Integrity Monitoring
Binary Authorization
Workload Identity
Network Policy
questions.explanation: Shielded GKE Nodes use Secure Boot to verify the node's boot components are signed and unmodified, vTPM for measured boot, and Integrity Monitoring to detect boot-time tampering. This protects the node infrastructure from rootkits and firmware-level attacks.

questions.question: An organization wants to implement a software supply chain security framework for their Google Cloud workloads. Which combination of services provides end-to-end supply chain security?
options: Cloud Build for verified builds, Artifact Registry for secure storage with vulnerability scanning, Binary Authorization for deploy-time verification, and Software Delivery Shield for overall visibility
GitHub Actions with manual image review
Cloud Build with no additional security controls
Third-party CI/CD with self-signed attestations
questions.explanation: End-to-end supply chain security requires: verified builds (Cloud Build with SLSA provenance), secure artifact storage (Artifact Registry with vulnerability scanning), deploy-time verification (Binary Authorization requiring attestations), and Software Delivery Shield for unified supply chain security posture management.

questions.question: A company has a regulatory requirement that their Cloud KMS keys must never be used outside of a specific VPC. How can this be enforced?
options: Use VPC Service Controls to create a perimeter around the Cloud KMS project, restricting key operations to originate only from within the allowed VPC
Use IAM Conditions based on IP address
Create a firewall rule blocking KMS API access
Use Cloud NAT for KMS access
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls can include Cloud KMS within a service perimeter, restricting all KMS operations (encrypt, decrypt, sign) to originate from within the perimeter boundary. Combined with Access Context Manager levels, this ensures keys can only be used from authorized network locations.
